Name: Aaron Bernstein
Initital self reflection: When this change was made, I noticed that the MSE test loss was much higher and the number of QB's the model incorrectly evaluated went up. I believe this is because we are testing on a new set of QB's every time so we do not give the model time to learn.
GenAI Guidance: train_test_split has a parameter shuffle that defaults to True. When shuffle=True, the function randomly permutes the rows before splitting them into 80% train and 20% test.
New reflection: The reason that the MSE test loss went up and we evaluated QB's at a poorer rate was because the model would train on the first 80% of QB's, never seeing the last 20%. Since my sheet was sorted by height, the model never saw a short QB leading to innacurate results during testing

Name: Aaron Bernstein
Initital self reflection: I believe that the reason the test loss was so unbelievably high was due to switching our method of error calculation from Mean squared error to BCE
GenAI Guidance: You swapped the loss function from nn.MSELoss() to nn.BCELoss(). These two losses are designed for fundamentally different problems:
New reflection: The reason that our test loss went up so much is because we switched from mean squared error loss to Binary Cross-Entropy which is designed for binary classification. If our model evaluated QB's by saying "good" or "bad", then BCE would work much better

Name: Aaron Bernstein
Initital self reflection: We are changing the activation function which will therefore result in different displacement of values defining what is a good and bad QB
GenAI Guidance: What the change actually does:You swapped the final activation from Sigmoid to ReLU. These two functions have completely different output ranges and shapes:
New reflection: The sigmoid function crushes number in between 0 and 1 while the ReLU function crunches numbers between 0 and infinity

Name: Aaron Bernstein
Initital self reflection: The code runs but the predicted value for every QB is 0. I honestly cannot even think of why this would occur, to me it seems that a higher learning rate would make the model learn more
GenAI Guidance:
What the change actually does:
The learning rate controls how big a step the optimizer takes in the direction the gradient points during each weight update.
New reflection: Learning rate is the size of each step the optimizer takes; setting it too high (5.0 instead of 0.01) makes the model leap past the loss minimum every update, weights explode toward infinity, sigmoid then squashes everything to 0, so every QB predicts the same value.

Name: Aaron Bernstein
Initital self reflection: Every test epoch will affect the next one since we do not reset the values, which is why every value is the same
GenAI Guidance: You're directionally right but too vague. "Affect the next one" doesn't explain how or why, and it doesn't show you understand what optimizer.zero_grad() does mechanically.
New reflection: PyTorch adds new gradients on top of old ones by default; optimizer.zero_grad() is what wipes them. Without it, gradients keep stacking every epoch, so the optimizer's step size grows without bound

Name: Aaron Bernstein
Initital self reflection: I believe that the .T could be applying a transpose matrix to the transformed matrix reversing the values given to each QB
GenAI Guidance: Close-ish in vocabulary (you said "transpose"), but the mechanism you're describing isn't right. "Reversing the values given to each QB" isn't what transpose does, and the bug isn't about reversal.
New reflection: StandardScaler operates per column, so transposing the matrix before fitting makes it standardize each QB's stats relative to their own other stats (mixing height inches with completion percentages), instead of standardizing each feature across all QBs. Example of this being that Patrick Mahomes speed is compared to his height, not other QB's speeds

Name: Aaron Bernstein
Initital self reflection: An error is caused due to the values used in the training data for X being too long
GenAI Guidance: You misread the error. "Long" in the error message is not about length, it's the name of a data type in PyTorch (specifically, int64). Same way Float means float32 and Double means float64. PyTorch uses the names "Long" and "Float" the way numpy uses dtype labels.
New reflection: Changing the input tensor to int64 (which PyTorch calls "Long") makes random sampling, weight multiplication, and gradient computation all fail because neural networks fundamentally require floating-point arithmetic, not integers.

Name: Aaron Bernstein
Initital self reflection: The dimensions of our tensor now are incapable of shrinking into a single number (scalar) giving us an error
GenAI Guidance:
You're gesturing at the right area but the explanation is muddled. MSE doesn't "shrink" the model output to a scalar in the way you're describing, and the issue isn't about the model failing to produce a scalar.
What the change actually does:
Your model used to output 1 number per QB (a single Rating prediction). Now it outputs 5 numbers per QB.
New reflection: Changing the output layer from Linear(16, 1) to Linear(16, 5) makes the model produce 5 predictions per QB instead of 1, which doesn't match the single-value target shape, so MSE errors out

Name: Aaron Bernstein
Initital self reflection: The loss is negative, and since the minimum loss is 0, the model believes it evaluated perfectly and therefore does not do any training. Once testing comes, it is so unfamiliar with the QB's it simply gives them 0
GenAI Guidance: Partial credit: you noticed that negative loss is the issue, and you connected it to the model's behavior. But the explanation of why is wrong. The model isn't "satisfied with itself and refusing to train." It IS training, very actively, just in the wrong direction.
New reflection: Negating the loss before backprop flips every gradient, turning gradient descent into gradient ascent, so the optimizer actively maximizes MSE instead of minimizing it; the model converges to the worst possible weights, where predicting 0 for everyone produces the largest possible squared error against high-rated QBs.
